name: Zooids: Swarm User Interfaces
short_description: A tabletop interface of many small wheeled micro-robots that self-arrange as a reconfigurable physical display and tangible control.
long_description: Zooids introduced 'swarm user interfaces,' a class of human-computer interface made of many small (26mm) custom wheeled robots that collectively handle both display and interaction on a tabletop. A high-speed DLP structured-light projector tracks each robot via embedded photodiodes, while PID and reciprocal-velocity-obstacle controllers coordinate motion so the swarm can render data physically, act as movable handles, and respond to touch and manipulation. It matters for physical AI and interaction design as a foundational demonstration of distributed, shape- and arrangement-changing tangible interfaces where a population of actuated objects becomes the medium of display and control.
ai_note: Coordination relies on PID controllers, state machines, and reciprocal velocity obstacle path planning, with optical tracking; no machine learning or AI is involved.

images.caption: A swarm of Zooids micro-robots arranged on a tabletop as a reconfigurable physical display.
images.alt_text: Many small cylindrical wheeled robots clustered on a flat surface, forming a pattern as a tangible swarm display.

sources.title: Zooids: Building Blocks for Swarm User Interfaces
